items.q: Where do plants get most of their biomass from?
items.correct: From carbon dioxide in the air — the carbon in plant tissue comes mostly from CO2 fixed during photosynthesis.
items.misconception: Plants get most of their mass from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass directly from sunlight, which has weight.
items.why: Roots in soil look like the source of food/mass, but actually most plant dry mass is carbon from atmospheric CO2.

items.q: Why are food chains usually limited to about 4 or 5 trophic levels?
items.correct: Only ~10 percent of energy is transferred between trophic levels; eventually too little energy remains to support another level.
items.misconception: There aren't enough types of animals to fill more trophic levels.
items.random_wrong: Larger animals always eat the next ones up, and there aren't enough giant animals.
items.why: Students don't grasp the energy-loss aspect of food chains; they think the limit is biological diversity rather than thermodynamic efficiency.

items.q: What is the main role of mitochondria in a cell?
items.correct: To carry out aerobic respiration, producing ATP that cells use as an energy currency.
items.misconception: Mitochondria produce energy by creating it from nothing.
items.random_wrong: Mitochondria store sunlight and release it for photosynthesis.
items.why: The phrase 'powerhouse of the cell' makes students think energy is created by mitochondria, missing that they transfer energy from glucose to ATP.

items.q: Is energy recycled in an ecosystem the way nutrients are?
items.correct: No — nutrients (like carbon and nitrogen) are recycled, but energy flows through and is lost as heat; it is not recycled.
items.misconception: Energy is recycled around an ecosystem just like carbon and nitrogen.
items.random_wrong: Energy is created by producers and stored forever.
items.why: Students extend nutrient cycling to energy, but energy enters as sunlight, flows one way through food chains, and leaves as heat.

items.q: Do plants get their carbon for growth from the soil?
items.correct: No — plants take in carbon as carbon dioxide from the air during photosynthesis; soil supplies water and minerals.
items.misconception: Plants absorb carbon from the soil through their roots.
items.random_wrong: Plants gain most of their mass from sunlight.
items.why: Roots suggest the soil is the source, but the carbon skeleton of a plant comes from atmospheric CO₂ fixed in photosynthesis.

items.q: Roughly what % of energy transfers between trophic levels?
items.why: Correct answer: 10%. “1%” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Which organisms convert nitrogen gas to nitrates?
items.why: Correct answer: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria. “Plants” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Producers take in 10000 kJ. Roughly how much reaches the primary consumer (kJ)?
items.why: Exact answer: 1000. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
